title: Wood Mackenzie: Falling fertility rates threaten long-term energy demand as global population could peak at 8.9 billion by 2053
gist: Wood Mackenzie warns accelerating declines in global fertility rates are a structural, not tail, risk to long-term energy demand, with a UN low-birth-rate scenario placing peak population at 8.9 billion in 2053 before contracting to 7.0 billion by 2100, arguing demographics belongs in the core scenario of every long-range energy model while electrification, renewables, and AI keep critical-minerals demand robust.

themes.theme: Demographics as a core scenario risk

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's Head of Economics argues shrinking workforces from falling fertility mean slower GDP growth with direct consequences for energy demand, and that this belongs in the core scenario of every long-range model rather than being treated as a tail risk.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: A leading energy consultancy is elevating demographic decline into base-case demand modeling, signaling lower long-run baselines.
insights.quote: This is not a tail risk. It belongs in the core scenario of every long-range model the industry relies on.

insights.claim: The global fertility rate fell from 2.6 births per woman in 2007 to 2.2 in 2025, perilously close to the 2.1 replacement ratio, identified as a structural risk for long-term energy demand.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Sub-replacement fertility is the quantitative anchor for downgrading long-term demand forecasts.
insights.quote: The global fertility rate fell to 2.2 births per woman in 2025, perilously close to the 2.1 replacement ratio needed to keep the population stable
insights.locator: The global fertility rate fell to 2.2 births

insights.claim: China's birth rate fell to 5.6 births per 1,000 people in 2025, the lowest ever recorded, and its population contracted by 3.4 million to 1.40 billion, 9.6 million below the UN's 2024 projection.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: China already undershooting UN forecasts validates the thesis that current demand models are too optimistic.
insights.quote: China's population contracted by 3.4 million people last year and now stands at 1.40 billion, some 9.6 million below the UN's 2024 projection

insights.claim: A UN low-birth-rate scenario places peak global population at 8.9 billion in 2053 followed by contraction to 7.0 billion by 2100, with a new UN World Population Prospects report expected in July 2026 likely to prompt reassessment of long-range demand projections.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: The July 2026 UN report is a discrete catalyst that could reset energy demand assumptions industry-wide.
insights.quote: a UN low-birth-rate scenario placing peak global population at 8.9 billion in 2053, followed by a contraction to 7.0 billion by 2100
insights.locator: Wood Mackenzie has published analysis warning

insights.claim: Even under a pessimistic demographic scenario, global population would still increase by around 700 million by 2060, and vast unmet energy needs across Asia and Africa plus electrification keep most demand drivers robust.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: The demographic risk is a post-2060 story; near-to-mid-term energy demand thesis remains intact.
insights.quote: Even under a pessimistic demographic scenario, global population would still increase by around 700 million people by 2060.
insights.locator: Even under a pessimistic demographic scenario

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie forecasts global primary energy consumption rising 8% to peak at 717 exajoules in 2035 before declining to 672 EJ by 2060, while electricity consumption doubles to 71 petawatt-hours.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Electricity doubling while primary energy peaks quantifies the electrification and decoupling from hydrocarbons.
insights.quote: peaking at 717 exajoules (EJ) in 2035 before declining to 672 EJ by 2060. Electricity consumption is projected to double to 71 petawatt-hours
insights.locator: Wood Mackenzie forecasts global primary energy consumption

insights.claim: Shrinking workforces increase the incentive to invest in AI-driven automation, embedding upside for electricity and critical minerals while creating a structural headwind for hydrocarbon molecules.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Demographic decline structurally favors electrons and critical minerals over oil and gas.
insights.quote: Shrinking workforces increase the incentive to invest in AI-driven automation, embedding upside for electricity and critical minerals while creating a structural headwind for molecules.
insights.locator: A lower population trajectory would, however, exacerbate

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's Head of Energy Transition argues a lower population does not diminish the draw on critical minerals, as electrification, renewables and AI adoption create unprecedented demand while accelerating the shift away from hydrocarbons.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Critical-minerals demand is decoupled from population, supporting a durable bull case for mining and battery materials.
insights.quote: A lower population does not diminish the draw on critical minerals. Electrification, renewables and AI adoption create unprecedented demand for those resources while accelerating the structural shift away from hydrocarbons.
insights.locator: A lower population does not diminish the draw

insights.claim: Automation could concentrate wealth among capital owners at the expense of labour, a distributional risk that limits consumer demand.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Inequality and weaker consumer demand are the second-order risks capping the automation growth story.
insights.quote: automation could concentrate wealth among capital owners at the expense of labour, limiting consumer demand
insights.locator: There is also a distributional risk

insights.claim: The capital to adapt energy systems exists now, and the question is whether governments deploy it decisively before the investment window narrows after 2060, with AI-driven productivity gains potentially offsetting the growth drag if smart, clean infrastructure is built while capital is available.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Frames grid and clean-energy capex as a time-limited opportunity; delay compounds the demographic drag.
insights.quote: The capital exists now. The question is whether governments move decisively enough to deploy it before the window narrows after 2060.
insights.locator: A lower population does not diminish the draw

insights.claim: An earlier and lower global population peak is now viable, and whether it proves a headwind or a catalyst for the next commodity super-cycle depends on how the world manages demographics, economic growth and technology.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Demographics is a swing variable for commodity super-cycle positioning.
insights.quote: Whether it proves a headwind or a catalyst for the next commodity super-cycle will depend on how the world manages the relationship between demographics, economic growth and technology.
insights.locator: An earlier and lower global population peak is now viable
